title: Technology Briefing: Cisco used AI to write security incident reports, with mixed results
summary: Cisco's recent experiment with AI-generated security incident reports serves as a microcosm for the broader challenges of automating critical infrastructure. While the initiative aims to streamline incident response, the mixed results underscore the limitations of generative models in high-stakes security contexts. Thi

body.text: The recent deployment of artificial intelligence within Cisco's security operations centers presents a fascinating case study in the friction between automation and accuracy. Organizations are eager to reduce the latency of incident response, yet the reality of generative models in safety-critical domains remains fraught with uncertainty. When Cisco turned to AI to draft security incident reports, the expectation was a seamless integration of technology and human oversight. The outcome, however, was a set of mixed results that forced a re-evaluation of how foundational security tools are built and deployed. This is not merely a story about one vendor's internal tools; it is a reflection of the industry-wide struggle to define the boundaries of trust in machine-generated content.
The Cisco Experiment and Operational Reality

The core issue identified during this rollout was not necessarily the lack of intelligence in the models, but the lack of context. Security incidents are not just data points; they are narratives involving specific actors, vulnerabilities, and potential consequences. When an AI model attempts to synthesize these elements, it often defaults to probability rather than verification. The mixed results suggest that while the AI may produce grammatically correct and logically structured text, the technical precision required for security documentation is still fragile. This operational reality highlights a significant gap between the hype of generative AI and the rigid requirements of enterprise security compliance.

body.alt: Image from source article for Dems slam Trump for making cybersecurity hold out the tin cup while splurging on ballroom and Jan. 6 'slush fund'
body.caption: Dems slam Trump for making cybersecurity hold out the tin cup while splurging on ballroom and Jan. 6 'slush fund'
body.sourceTitle: Dems slam Trump for making cybersecurity hold out the tin cup while splurging on ballroom and Jan. 6 'slush fund'

body.text: Furthermore, the reliance on AI for documentation introduces a dependency on the quality of the training data. If the underlying datasets contain biases or outdated threat intelligence, the reports generated will reflect those flaws. The mixed results indicate that the AI is capable of writing, but it is not yet capable of verifying the truth of the content it generates. This creates a new operational burden where human analysts must spend more time validating the AI's output than previously anticipated. The shift from manual drafting to AI-assisted drafting has changed the workflow, but it has not yet solved the problem of accountability.
Political Friction in Cybersecurity Funding

Cisco's internal struggles with AI are mirrored by the external political landscape governing cybersecurity infrastructure. There is a stark contrast in how different political factions view the allocation of resources for national security versus domestic spending. Recent signals indicate a divide where cybersecurity funding is viewed as a necessity that is often underfunded, described as holding out the tin cup. Meanwhile, other areas of spending are described as splurging on ballroom and slush funds. This disparity creates a volatile environment for companies like Cisco that rely on government contracts and public sector partnerships.

body.alt: Image from source article for Roundtables: Can AI Learn to Understand the World?
body.caption: Roundtables: Can AI Learn to Understand the World?
body.sourceTitle: Roundtables: Can AI Learn to Understand the World?

body.text: The political narrative suggests that cybersecurity is often deprioritized in favor of visible political projects. This has operational consequences for the technology sector. Companies must navigate a landscape where infrastructure investment is inconsistent and policy direction is unpredictable. The focus on slush funds and ballroom spending contrasts sharply with the need for robust, long-term security infrastructure. This friction means that companies cannot rely on stable funding streams to support the expensive experimentation required to keep AI tools accurate and secure. The result is a pressure to cut costs, which may inadvertently accelerate the adoption of less reliable AI tools.
The Challenge of Grounded Understanding

Moving beyond the immediate operational issues, we must consider the deeper theoretical questions regarding how AI models function. The inquiry into whether AI can learn to understand the world touches on the fundamental problem of grounding. Current models excel at pattern recognition and text generation, but they lack a true understanding of causality and physical reality. When an AI writes a security report, it does not understand the threat it describes; it only predicts the next likely word based on previous examples. This distinction is critical when discussing the reliability of automated systems in safety-critical environments.

body.alt: Image from source article for Scaling creativity in the age of AI
body.caption: Scaling creativity in the age of AI
body.sourceTitle: Scaling creativity in the age of AI

body.text: The platform shifts in how we interact with information are profound. We are moving from a model where humans verify information to a model where machines generate information that humans must verify. This inversion of workflow places a new burden on human analysts. The technology review discussions suggest that the current trajectory of AI development is not yet aligned with the need for deep understanding. The focus remains on scaling creativity and output volume, but the underlying mechanism of understanding remains elusive. Until this gap is bridged, the mixed results from Cisco will likely remain the norm rather than the exception.
Scaling Creativity and Product Strategy

In the realm of product strategy, the ability to scale creativity is a double-edged sword. Companies are using AI to generate marketing copy, code, and design assets at unprecedented rates. This efficiency drives growth, but it also dilutes the human touch that often defines brand identity. The scaling of creativity in the age of AI means that the value of human innovation is increasingly measured against the output of machines. This creates a competitive dynamic where the most innovative companies are those that can integrate AI without losing the human nuance that customers value.

From a cloud economics perspective, the cost of generating content is dropping, but the cost of managing the output is rising. Companies are facing the challenge of integrating these tools into existing workflows without creating new bottlenecks. The infrastructure required to support large language models is substantial, and the cloud economics are shifting towards a model where compute is a primary variable cost. This means that the decision to deploy AI tools is not just about the quality of the output, but the cost of the infrastructure required to maintain it. The mixed results from Cisco's security reports reflect this broader economic reality where the cost of failure is high.
Societal Trust and the Spiritual Question

The implications of AI extend beyond technology and into the realm of societal trust. There is a growing trend where individuals are turning to AI for advice on matters traditionally reserved for human experts. A notable signal indicates that half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice. This level of trust suggests that the public is comfortable delegating complex, high-stakes decisions to algorithms. This trend is concerning for the technology industry because it raises questions about the nature of truth and the role of human judgment. When people trust AI for spiritual guidance, they are implicitly accepting the model's output as valid.

This trust is a product of the model's ability to mimic empathy and understanding. However, the mechanism behind this mimicry is statistical correlation, not genuine insight. The operational consequence is that users may not question the output, leading to the propagation of errors. In the context of security, this means that if an AI hallucinates a threat level, the trust placed in that data could lead to real-world harm. The challenge for the industry is to build systems that are transparent about their limitations, even when the output is compelling and persuasive.
The three critical risks identified in the Cisco rollout are:

items: The potential for hallucinated technical details in security documentation
The reduction in human oversight required during incident response
The lack of accountability when automated systems make errors

body.text: As the industry continues to navigate these waters, the focus must shift from the capabilities of the models to the reliability of the systems in which they are embedded. The mixed results from Cisco are a warning sign that the current trajectory of AI development is not aligned with the need for deep understanding. We are seeing a proliferation of tools that scale creativity and efficiency, but the foundational trust required for critical infrastructure remains fragile. The political friction regarding cybersecurity funding further complicates the ability to invest in the verification mechanisms needed to ensure these tools work correctly. The path forward requires a recalibration of expectations, acknowledging that AI is a powerful tool but not a replacement for human judgment. The goal is not to eliminate the human element, but to ensure that the human element remains central to the decision-making process. We must ensure that the technology serves the goal of security rather than becoming a source of new vulnerabilities.
Why it matters for builders

For technology operators, the important question is whether the news changes platform leverage, infrastructure cost, security posture, developer behavior, or the tradeoff between control and speed. Those mechanisms determine whether a headline becomes a real operating constraint.

The lead signal is Cisco used AI to write security incident reports, with mixed results. Leo would not read it as a standalone item. He would place it beside the rest of the source set and ask which assumption is being tested: demand, cost, trust, policy, infrastructure capacity, or the willingness of customers and investors to keep paying for the current narrative.

The current source mix points to several connected signals rather than one isolated story: Cisco used AI to write security incident reports, with mixed results; Dems slam Trump for making cybersecurity hold out the tin cup while splurging on ballroom and Jan. 6 'slush fund'; Roundtables: Can AI Learn to Understand the World?; Scaling creativity in the age of AI; Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice. Read together, they give Leo a broader map of where attention is moving and which assumptions deserve a second look.
Second-order effects

The second-order question is where complexity moves. A tool can make one team faster while pushing cost, governance, or operational risk onto another team. The useful analysis follows that transfer instead of stopping at the vendor's preferred story.

Leo's operating lens is to ask what a careful builder would do differently on Monday morning. If the answer is a clearer dependency map, a different vendor boundary, a security review, or a more conservative architecture choice, the headline has practical weight.
What to watch next

The next useful check is where the pressure shows up first: procurement, architecture reviews, incident response, margins, or product roadmaps. That is usually where the strategic meaning becomes visible before it appears in a polished executive narrative.

The practical discipline is to avoid treating the freshest headline as the whole story. The stronger read is slower and more mechanical: identify the constraint, watch whether it spreads, and keep revising the map as better evidence arrives.
